## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER concatenate or interpolate user input into SQL/NoSQL/LDAP queries. MUST use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`) or prepared statements. Example: use `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1", [id])` instead of `query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id)`. Applies to SQL, NoSQL, LDAP, and OS commands.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. Use proper certificate chains in all HTTP, database, and API connections. For local dev, use mkcert to generate locally-trusted certificates instead of disabling verification.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256+ for stored sensitive data. Hash passwords with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2—NEVER MD5/SHA. NEVER implement custom crypto; use established libraries (crypto, libsodium). Example: `bcrypt.hash(password, 12)` for password storage.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
MUST verify the authenticated caller is authorized for the specific resource/operation. Check ownership on `/:id` routes, require role on admin endpoints. Server-side enforcement only—never rely on client-side guards. BOLA/IDOR is unacceptable.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, or session management. MUST use project's auth system or proven provider (Auth0, Firebase, Cognito, Clerk). Custom auth invites timing attacks and token vulnerabilities.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, or `git push --force` without explicit confirmation. MUST use soft deletes over hard deletes and provide rollback scripts for migrations. Example: `DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ? AND confirmed_deletion = true` with user confirmation first.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
ALWAYS use migration tools (Drizzle, Prisma, Alembic); NEVER run ad-hoc SQL on production. Every migration MUST have a rollback. Example: create `migrations/001_add_users_table.sql` with up/down scripts, never ALTER TABLE directly.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER expose stack traces, SQL, file paths, or internal IDs in responses. MUST return: `{"error":{"code":"SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE","message":"safe message","requestId":"..."}}`. Route all errors through centralized handler.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log, expose in API responses, store in queries, or display PII (names, emails, SSNs, phone numbers, tokens). Mask sensitive fields with hashes or redaction. Example: log user_id instead of user_email; return {'user_id': '123'} not {'email': 'user@example.com'}.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### secrets management
NEVER commit secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) to code or logs. ALWAYS use environment variables or vaults. Scan for exposed secrets on every commit; revoke immediately if found.

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks, bare `except: pass`, `catch(e) {}`, or `.catch(() => {})`. Every catch block MUST handle the error (retry/fallback/log with context), re-throw it, or include a comment explaining why it's safe to ignore. Example: `catch(e) { logger.error('Payment failed', {orderId, error: e}); throw e; }`

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
MUST include tests for all new functions, endpoints, and behavioral changes. Minimum: happy path test, one error case, one boundary condition. Use the project's existing test framework and directory. Example: for a new payment endpoint, test success, invalid amount, and zero/negative values.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all inputs (query, body, params) with Zod before processing. Example: `z.object({reference_id: z.string().uuid()})`. Return 400 with validation error, never proceed with invalid data.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
MUST access all databases through repository classes (e.g., WebhookRepository, ChargeRepository). NEVER use Prisma directly in service code. Example: call webhookRepository.findById(id) instead of prisma.webhook.findUnique({where: {id}}).

## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
ALWAYS log with: timestamp, level, context (userId, requestId), message, error stack. Format: `{"level":"error","requestId":"...","error":"...","code":500}`. Never log sensitive data (passwords, tokens). Use consistent error codes across endpoints.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### tailwind css usage
NEVER use inline styles—ALWAYS use Tailwind CSS classes. Example: `className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/50 flex items-center justify-center"` instead of style objects. Tailwind is the exclusive styling method for this project.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
ALWAYS return errors as `{ error: { code: "ERROR_CODE", message: "description" } }`. Never return raw exceptions or unstructured messages. See API standards for complete format.
